sentences.base_language: Xiao Ming signed up for a cooking class at the culinary school, something he had wanted to do for years.
sentences.target_language: 小明在烹饪学校报名参加了一门烹饪课，这是他多年来一直想做的事。
sentences.romanized: Xiǎo Míng zài pēngrèn xuéxiào bàomíng cānjiā le yī mén pēngrèn kè, zhè shì tā duō nián lái yīzhí xiǎng zuò de shì.
sentences.grammarNote:
**报名参加** = to sign up and join/participate: **报名** (bàomíng, to register/sign up) + **参加** (cānjiā, to join/attend). These two verbs often appear together in sequence — the first describes the act of registering, the second describes attending: **报名**参加比赛 (sign up for a competition), **报名**参加培训 (register for training). In English this is one phrase ('sign up for'); in Chinese both steps are made explicit.
**一门** as a measure word for courses: **门** (mén, door/gate) is the standard measure word for academic courses and subjects: **一门**课 (one course), **两门**外语 (two foreign languages), **三门**考试 (three exams). Knowing subject-specific measure words is essential: **一门** for courses, **一堂** (yī táng) for individual lessons/class sessions, **一节课** (yī jié kè) for a class period.

sentences.base_language: When he arrived, the instructor — an experienced chef — welcomed the group warmly.
sentences.target_language: 他到达时，教练——一位经验丰富的厨师——热情地欢迎了大家。
sentences.romanized: Tā dàodá shí, jiàoliàn — yī wèi jīngyàn fēngfù de chúshī — rèqíng de huānyíng le dàjiā.
sentences.grammarNote:
**经验丰富** (jīngyàn fēngfù) = rich in experience, experienced: **经验** (experience) + **丰富** (abundant/rich). This is a common Chinese compound descriptor: subject + attribute in apposition: **经验**丰富的医生 (an experienced doctor), **知识**丰富 (knowledgeable). **丰富** (fēngfù) = rich, abundant, and pairs widely with nouns: **资源**丰富 (resource-rich), **营养**丰富 (nutritious, rich in nutrients).
**热情地 + verb** = to do something warmly/enthusiastically: **热情** (rèqíng, warm, enthusiastic) + **地** (de, adverbial marker). The structure **adjective + 地 + verb** is how Chinese forms adverbs: **慢慢**地走 (walk slowly), **认真**地学 (study seriously), **热情**地欢迎 (welcome warmly). **地** (de, adverbial) is one of three structural particles: **的** (adjectival), **地** (adverbial), **得** (complement).

sentences.base_language: The instructor announced that they would make a traditional three-course meal from scratch.
sentences.target_language: 教练宣布，他们将从零开始制作一顿传统的三道菜正餐。
sentences.romanized: Jiàoliàn xuānbù, tāmen jiāng cóng líng kāishǐ zhìzuò yī dùn chuántǒng de sān dào cài zhèngcān.
sentences.grammarNote:
**从零开始** (cóng líng kāishǐ) = to start from zero, from scratch: **从** (from) + **零** (zero) + **开始** (begin). This is one of the most common Chinese expressions for beginning with no prior preparation: **从零开始**学中文 (learn Chinese from scratch), **一切从零开始** (start everything anew). Compare: **从头开始** (cóng tóu kāishǐ, start from the beginning/head — slightly different, implying restarting rather than true zero-knowledge).
**道** as a measure word for courses/dishes: **道** (dào, way/path/course) is the measure word for dishes in a meal: **三道菜** (three courses/dishes), **一道主菜** (one main course), **五道菜的宴席** (a five-course banquet). Compare: **盘** (pán, plate — counts by serving plate: **一盘**炒饭, a plate of fried rice) and **份** (fèn, portion: **一份**套餐, a set meal).

sentences.base_language: Xiao Ming had never cooked anything more complex than instant noodles, so he felt both excited and nervous.
sentences.target_language: 小明从来没做过比泡面更复杂的东西，所以他既兴奋又紧张。
sentences.romanized: Xiǎo Míng cónglái méi zuò guò bǐ pào miàn gèng fùzá de dōngxi, suǒyǐ tā jì xīngfèn yòu jǐnzhāng.
sentences.grammarNote:
**既…又…** (jì…yòu…) = both… and…: **既**兴奋**又**紧张 (both excited and nervous). This structure joins two simultaneous states or qualities: **既**聪明**又**勤奋 (both clever and hardworking), **既**便宜**又**好吃 (both cheap and delicious). Compare: **又…又…** (yòu…yòu…, also 'both…and…' — more colloquial): **又**高**又**帅 (both tall and handsome). **既…又…** is slightly more formal and often used for contrasting or complex qualities.
**从来没…过** = have never (experienced): **从来没**做**过** (have never done/made). **从来** (cónglái, ever, at any time) + **没** (negative) + verb + **过** (guò, experiential aspect marker). The **过** marks the lack of a life experience: **从来没去过**北京 (have never been to Beijing), **从来没见过**这种事 (have never seen such a thing). **过** for experience pairs with **从来没** to express a complete absence of prior experience.

sentences.base_language: First, the instructor demonstrated how to hold a knife correctly to avoid cutting their fingers.
sentences.target_language: 首先，教练示范了正确握刀的方式，以免切到手指。
sentences.romanized: Shǒuxiān, jiàoliàn shìfàn le zhèngquè wò dāo de fāngshì, yǐmiǎn qiē dào shǒuzhǐ.
sentences.grammarNote:
**以免** (yǐmiǎn) = in order to avoid, so as not to: **以** (yǐ, in order to) + **免** (miǎn, avoid/exempt). **以免** introduces a negative consequence being avoided: **以免**受伤 (so as not to get hurt), **以免**误会 (to avoid misunderstanding), 请带伞，**以免**淋雨 (bring an umbrella so you don't get rained on). More formal than **免得** (miǎnde, colloquial equivalent): **免得**切到手 (so you don't cut your hand).
**切到** = cut and reach/touch (resultative): **切** (qiē, to cut) + **到** (dào, reach/contact). The resultative complement **到** indicates that the action of cutting reaches its target (in this case, the fingers — an unwanted result): **打到**人 (hit someone), **碰到**了 (touched/bumped into), **切到**手 (accidentally cut one's hand). **到** as a resultative complement is one of the most versatile in Chinese.

sentences.base_language: She explained that most kitchen accidents happen because people do not respect the blade.
sentences.target_language: 她解释说，大多数厨房事故的发生是因为人们不尊重刀刃。
sentences.romanized: Tā jiěshì shuō, dàduōshù chúfáng shìgù de fāshēng shì yīnwèi rénmen bù zūnzhòng dāorèn.
sentences.grammarNote:
**解释说** (jiěshì shuō) = explained (saying): **解释** (jiěshì, to explain) + **说** (shuō, say). Pairing a communication verb with **说** is very natural in Chinese narrative: **告诉他说** (told him that…), **提醒大家说** (reminded everyone that…), **宣布说** (announced that…). The **说** introduces the content of what was communicated, functioning like a quotation marker even without direct speech.
**Topic-comment structure**: **大多数厨房事故的发生是因为…** — the topic (大多数厨房事故的发生, the occurrence of most kitchen accidents) is placed at the front, and the comment (是因为…, is because…) follows. Chinese frequently fronts a noun phrase as the topic: **这道菜**，我不喜欢 (this dish, I don't like it). Here the abstract noun **发生** (occurrence) is nominalized by **的** to become the topic of the sentence.

sentences.base_language: Xiao Ming practiced chopping onions and his eyes began to water almost immediately.
sentences.target_language: 小明练习切洋葱，眼睛几乎立刻就开始流泪了。
sentences.romanized: Xiǎo Míng liànxí qiē yángcōng, yǎnjing jīhū lìkè jiù kāishǐ liú lèi le.
sentences.grammarNote:
**几乎** (jīhū) = almost, nearly: **几乎立刻** (almost immediately). **几乎** expresses near-completeness or near-immediacy: **几乎**所有人 (almost everyone), **几乎**没有 (almost none), **几乎**不可能 (almost impossible). Compare: **差不多** (chàbuduō, almost/about — more colloquial and often interchangeable): **差不多**立刻 (almost right away). **几乎** is slightly more literary or precise.
**就…了** for emphasis on immediacy: **就开始流泪了** — **就** (jiù) + verb + **了** (le) signals that something happened quickly or sooner than expected: 他**就**来了 (he came right away), 吃了一口**就**知道了 (knew after just one bite). Here **几乎立刻就** stacks three immediacy markers for emphasis: 几乎 (almost) + 立刻 (immediately) + 就 (right then). The **了** at the sentence end marks the change of state (eyes that weren't watering, now are).

sentences.base_language: The other students laughed kindly, and the instructor said it meant the onions were very fresh.
sentences.target_language: 其他同学善意地笑了，教练说这说明洋葱非常新鲜。
sentences.romanized: Qítā tóngxué shànyì de xiào le, jiàoliàn shuō zhè shuōmíng yángcōng fēicháng xīnxiān.
sentences.grammarNote:
**善意** (shànyì) = goodwill, kindly intent: **善意地**笑了 (laughed with good intentions/kindly). **善意** (good + intention) is the opposite of **恶意** (èyì, malice, ill intent): **善意**的批评 (kindly-meant criticism), **恶意**攻击 (malicious attack). Using **善意地** as an adverbial modifies the manner of laughing to make clear no mockery was intended — a nuanced word choice.
**说明** (shuōmíng) = to show, to indicate, to demonstrate: **这说明**洋葱新鲜 (this shows the onions are fresh). **说明** is a versatile verb meaning 'to explain or prove by showing': 这**说明**了问题 (this illustrates the problem), 成绩**说明**一切 (results speak for themselves). As a noun: **说明书** (shuōmíngshū, instruction manual). Compare: **表明** (biǎomíng, to indicate/make clear — more formal), **证明** (zhèngmíng, to prove — implies stronger evidence).

sentences.base_language: For the main course, they prepared a slow-braised dish with seasonal vegetables and tender meat.
sentences.target_language: 在主菜环节，他们准备了一道用时令蔬菜和嫩肉慢炖的菜肴。
sentences.romanized: Zài zhǔcài huánjié, tāmen zhǔnbèi le yī dào yòng shílìng shūcài hé nèn ròu màn dùn de càiyáo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**慢炖** (màn dùn) = to slow-braise, to simmer slowly: **慢** (slow) + **炖** (dùn, to braise/stew by simmering in liquid). **炖** is a specific Chinese cooking method — simmering ingredients in water or broth at low heat for a long time, producing a tender, deeply flavored result. Key Chinese cooking methods to know: **炒** (chǎo, stir-fry), **炸** (zhá, deep-fry), **蒸** (zhēng, steam), **炖** (dùn, braise/stew), **煮** (zhǔ, boil), **烤** (kǎo, roast/grill).
**时令** (shílìng) = seasonal, in season: **时** (time/season) + **令** (order/rule). **时令蔬菜** (seasonal vegetables) refers to produce at its natural peak season: **时令**水果 (seasonal fruit), **应季**食材 (in-season ingredients — near synonym). **时令** is preferred in slightly elevated or culinary contexts. Compare the everyday equivalent: **应季** (yìngjì, in season) or simply saying **现在**正是**XX**的季节 (now is the season for XX).

sentences.base_language: The instructor walked around, tasting each person's dish and offering quiet suggestions.
sentences.target_language: 教练在教室里走动，品尝每个人的菜，轻声给出建议。
sentences.romanized: Jiàoliàn zài jiàoshì lǐ zǒudòng, pǐncháng měi gè rén de cài, qīngshēng gěi chū jiànyì.
sentences.grammarNote:
**品尝** (pǐncháng) = to taste, to sample with discernment: **品** (pǐn, to taste/appreciate) + **尝** (cháng, to taste). **品尝** implies tasting with attention and judgment, not just eating: **品尝**美酒 (taste fine wine), **品尝**各地美食 (sample regional cuisines). Compare: **尝** alone (to taste/try, casual: 你**尝**一口 — try a bite), **品** alone (to appreciate/savor — more literary).
**给出** (gěi chū) = to offer, to give out: **给** (give) + **出** (out, directional complement). **给出**建议 (offer/give suggestions), **给出**答案 (provide an answer), **给出**解释 (give an explanation). The directional complement **出** implies the advice or information is being produced and delivered outward. Compare: **提出** (tíchū, raise/put forward — used for proposals, questions): **提出**问题 (raise a question), **提出**建议 (put forward a suggestion — slightly more formal).

sentences.base_language: Xiao Ming accidentally added too much salt but the instructor said it was an easy mistake to fix.
sentences.target_language: 小明不小心加了太多盐，但教练说这种错误很容易纠正。
sentences.romanized: Xiǎo Míng bù xiǎoxīn jiā le tài duō yán, dàn jiàoliàn shuō zhè zhǒng cuòwù hěn róngyì jiūzhèng.
sentences.grammarNote:
**不小心** (bù xiǎoxīn) = accidentally, carelessly: **不** (not) + **小心** (xiǎoxīn, careful). **不小心**加了太多盐 (accidentally added too much salt). **不小心** is the standard colloquial expression for doing something by accident or through inattention: **不小心**摔倒了 (accidentally fell down), **不小心**说错了 (accidentally said the wrong thing). Compare: **意外地** (yìwài de, unexpectedly — more formal/written) and **偶然** (ǒurán, by chance — implies randomness rather than carelessness).
**纠正** (jiūzhèng) = to correct, to rectify: **纠** (to correct/rectify) + **正** (straight/correct). **纠正**错误 (correct a mistake), **纠正**发音 (correct pronunciation), **纠正**姿势 (correct posture). **纠正** focuses on adjusting something that was wrong back to the right state. Compare: **改正** (gǎizhèng, to correct/amend — often used for written errors or one's own behaviour): **改正**错别字 (correct typos), **改正**坏习惯 (correct bad habits).

sentences.base_language: She showed him how to balance the flavors by adding a splash of vinegar and a pinch of sugar.
sentences.target_language: 她向小明示范了如何通过加入少许醋和一小撮糖来平衡味道。
sentences.romanized: Tā xiàng Xiǎo Míng shìfàn le rúhé tōngguò jiārù shǎo xǔ cù hé yī xiǎo cuō táng lái pínghéng wèidào.
sentences.grammarNote:
**通过…来…** (tōngguò…lái…) = by means of… to achieve…: **通过**加入醋和糖**来**平衡味道 (by adding vinegar and sugar to balance the flavors). This is a very common structural pattern: **通过** + [method] + **来** + [goal]. **通过**学习**来**进步 (improve through studying), **通过**练习**来**提高 (improve by practicing). **来** here is a purposive marker linking the method to the aim.
**少许** (shǎo xǔ) = a small amount, a dash/splash of: **少** (few/little) + **许** (allow/some). **少许**醋 (a splash of vinegar), **少许**盐 (a pinch of salt). **少许** is a measured, somewhat elevated culinary term. Compare informal alternatives: **一点儿** (yīdiǎnr, a little bit — everyday), **一点点** (yīdiǎndiǎn, a tiny bit — colloquial). **一小撮** (yī xiǎo cuō) = a small pinch: **撮** (cuō) is a measure word for things pinched between fingers, exactly as in English 'pinch'.

sentences.base_language: The aroma from the pot filled the kitchen and made everyone's mouth water.
sentences.target_language: 锅里散发出的香气弥漫了整个厨房，让所有人垂涎欲滴。
sentences.romanized: Guō lǐ sànfā chū de xiāngqì mímàn le zhěnggè chúfáng, ràng suǒyǒu rén chuí xián yù dī.
sentences.grammarNote:
**散发出** (sànfā chū) = to emit, to give off, to emanate: **散发** (diffuse/emit) + **出** (out, directional complement). **散发**出香气 (emit an aroma), **散发**出光芒 (radiate light), **散发**出魅力 (exude charm). The directional complement **出** emphasizes the outward movement of the emission. **弥漫** (mímàn) = to permeate, to fill (of smell, smoke, or mist spreading everywhere): 烟雾**弥漫** (smoke filled the air), 香气**弥漫**整个房间 (aroma filled the whole room).
**垂涎欲滴** (chuí xián yù dī) = to drool with desire, to make one's mouth water — a vivid four-character idiom: **垂** (droop/hang down) + **涎** (xián, saliva) + **欲** (about to) + **滴** (drip). Literally: saliva drooping as if about to drip. Used both literally (very appetizing food) and figuratively (desire for something: **垂涎**已久 — long coveted). A classic example of how Chinese idioms encode vivid physical imagery.

sentences.base_language: By the time the food was ready, Xiao Ming's arms were tired from all the stirring.
sentences.target_language: 等食物做好的时候，小明因为一直翻炒而手臂有些发酸了。
sentences.romanized: Děng shíwù zuò hǎo de shíhou, Xiǎo Míng yīnwèi yīzhí fānchǎo ér shǒubì yǒuxiē fā suān le.
literal: by the time the food was ready,
sentences.grammarNote:
**等…的时候** (děng…de shíhou) = by the time…, when…: **等**食物做好**的时候** (by the time the food was done). **等** here is a conjunction meaning 'by the time' or 'when (a future or expected event occurs)': **等**你回来的时候 (by the time you get back), **等**毕业了再说 (let's talk about it after graduation). This usage of **等** differs from its meaning as 'to wait': **等**一下 (wait a moment).
**发酸** (fā suān) = to become sore, to start aching: **发** (fā, to emit/start to) + **酸** (suān, sore/dull aching). **发** + sensation adjective = to start feeling that way: **发麻** (fā má, go numb), **发烫** (fā tàng, become burning hot), **发酸** (become sore/aching). This pattern describes the onset of a physical sensation. **翻炒** (fānchǎo) = to stir-fry with tossing: **翻** (flip/turn) + **炒** (stir-fry). The **翻** emphasizes the tossing motion.

sentences.base_language: For dessert, they made a simple egg tart that required careful attention to detail.
sentences.target_language: 在甜点环节，他们制作了一款简单的蛋挞，这需要非常细心。
sentences.romanized: Zài tiándiǎn huánjié, tāmen zhìzuò le yī kuǎn jiǎndān de dàn tà, zhè xūyào fēicháng xìxīn.
sentences.grammarNote:
**蛋挞** (dàn tà) = egg tart — a transliteration-plus-meaning compound: **蛋** (dàn, egg) + **挞** (tà, a phonetic borrowing from English 'tart'). **挞** is one of many Chinese characters used primarily for their sound in transliterations of foreign food names: **挞** in **蛋挞**, **芝士** (zhīshì, cheese — from Cantonese), **沙拉** (shālā, salad). Egg tarts are a beloved dim sum item with Portuguese and British colonial origins in Macau and Hong Kong.
**细心** (xìxīn) = careful, meticulous, attentive to detail: **细** (xì, fine/detailed) + **心** (xīn, heart/mind). **细心**地检查 (check carefully), 做事很**细心** (does things carefully). Compare: **小心** (xiǎoxīn, cautious/careful about danger: **小心**地雷! — Watch out for mines!), **耐心** (nàixīn, patient), **细心** (attentive to detail — about quality and thoroughness rather than caution about danger).

sentences.base_language: Xiao Ming felt proud when his tart came out of the oven perfectly golden.
sentences.target_language: 当小明的蛋挞从烤箱里出来时，颜色金黄完美，他感到非常自豪。
sentences.romanized: Dāng Xiǎo Míng de dàn tà cóng kǎoxiāng lǐ chūlái shí, yánsè jīnhuáng wánměi, tā gǎndào fēicháng zìháo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**金黄** (jīnhuáng) = golden yellow, golden: **金** (gold) + **黄** (yellow). **金黄**色 (golden color), **烤得金黄** (baked to a golden color), 秋天树叶变得**金黄** (autumn leaves turn golden). Chinese uses two-character color compounds for more nuanced shades: **天蓝** (sky blue), **墨绿** (ink green/dark green), **金黄** (golden yellow). These are more vivid and descriptive than single-character color words.
**自豪** (zìháo) = to feel proud, pride: **自** (self) + **豪** (grand/heroic). **感到**自豪 (feel proud), **为…感到自豪** (feel proud of…): 我为你**感到自豪** (I am proud of you). Compare: **骄傲** (jiāo'ào) — this can mean 'proud' positively (我很**骄傲**我做到了, I'm proud I did it) but also 'arrogant' negatively (他太**骄傲**了, he's too arrogant). **自豪** is always positive (pride in an achievement or identity), never negative.

sentences.base_language: The class sat together to eat the meal they had prepared, feeling a warm sense of accomplishment.
sentences.target_language: 大家坐在一起，品尝了自己亲手制作的这顿饭，心中充满了温暖的成就感。
sentences.romanized: Dàjiā zuò zài yīqǐ, pǐncháng le zìjǐ qīnshǒu zhìzuò de zhè dùn fàn, xīn zhōng chōngmǎn le wēnnuǎn de chéngjìugǎn.
sentences.grammarNote:
**亲手** (qīnshǒu) = with one's own hands, personally: **亲** (qīn, personally/in person) + **手** (shǒu, hand). **亲手**制作 (made by hand personally), **亲手**送上 (personally delivered). **亲** as a prefix intensifies the personal involvement: **亲眼**看见 (saw with one's own eyes), **亲耳**听到 (heard with one's own ears), **亲身**经历 (experienced personally). This class of expressions is very natural in Chinese narrative and adds warmth and authenticity.
**成就感** (chéngjìugǎn) = sense of accomplishment: **成就** (achievement) + **感** (gǎn, feeling/sense). The suffix **感** is highly productive for abstract emotional or physical sensations: **幸福感** (sense of happiness), **安全感** (sense of security), **使命感** (sense of mission), **满足感** (sense of satisfaction). Learning **感** as a suffix dramatically expands your vocabulary for discussing emotional states and subjective experiences.

sentences.base_language: Xiao Ming discovered that cooking was more satisfying than he had expected.
sentences.target_language: 小明发现，烹饪比他预想的更令人满足。
sentences.romanized: Xiǎo Míng fāxiàn, pēngrèn bǐ tā yùxiǎng de gèng lìng rén mǎnzú.
sentences.grammarNote:
**比…更…** (bǐ…gèng…) = even more… than…: **比**他预想的**更**令人满足 (more satisfying than he had expected). The standard comparison: **A 比 B + adj** (A is more adj than B): 今天**比**昨天**更**热 (today is even hotter than yesterday). Adding **更** (gèng, even more) after **比** emphasizes the degree of difference. Compare: **没有…那么…** (not as… as…): 没有他想象的那么难 (not as hard as he imagined) — the negative comparison form.
**令人满足** (lìng rén mǎnzú) = satisfying, to make people feel satisfied: **令** (lìng, to make/cause) + **人** (rén, people/one) + **满足** (mǎnzú, satisfied). **令人** + adj/verb is a formal, versatile pattern: **令人**惊叹 (astonishing), **令人**感动 (moving/touching), **令人**失望 (disappointing), **令人**满足 (satisfying). Compare the colloquial **让人**: **让人**满足 (makes one feel satisfied). **令人** is slightly more formal; **让人** is more conversational.

sentences.base_language: The instructor invited them all to return for the next class, which would focus on regional cuisines.
sentences.target_language: 教练邀请大家回来参加下一节课，那节课将专注于地方菜系。
sentences.romanized: Jiàoliàn yāoqǐng dàjiā huílái cānjiā xià yī jié kè, nà jié kè jiāng zhuānzhù yú dìfāng càixì.
sentences.grammarNote:
**菜系** (càixì) = cuisine system, regional culinary tradition: **菜** (dishes/cuisine) + **系** (xì, system/lineage). China's **八大菜系** (eight great cuisine traditions) include **粤菜** (Cantonese), **川菜** (Sichuan), **鲁菜** (Shandong), **苏菜** (Jiangsu), etc. **系** as a suffix creates 'system' or 'school' words: **体系** (system), **派系** (faction), **菜系** (culinary tradition). Understanding **菜系** is essential cultural knowledge for talking about Chinese food.
**专注于** (zhuānzhù yú) = to focus on, to concentrate on: **专注** (zhuānzhù, concentrated/focused) + **于** (yú, on/at — formal preposition). **专注于**研究 (focus on research), **专注于**工作 (concentrate on work). **于** (yú) is a formal preposition appearing in written and elevated registers: **用于** (used for), **关于** (concerning/about), **专注于** (focused on). In everyday speech, **专注**在…上 or **专心**做… are more colloquial.

sentences.base_language: Xiao Ming went home with a full stomach and a new passion, already planning what he would cook the following weekend.
sentences.target_language: 小明吃饱了肚子，怀揣着新发现的热情回了家，已经在心里盘算着下周末要做什么菜了。
sentences.romanized: Xiǎo Míng chī bǎo le dùzi, huái chuāi zhe xīn fāxiàn de rèqíng huí le jiā, yǐjīng zài xīnlǐ pánsuan zhe xià zhōumò yào zuò shénme cài le.
sentences.grammarNote:
**吃饱** (chī bǎo) = to eat one's fill, to eat until full: **吃** (eat) + **饱** (bǎo, full/satiated — resultative complement). **饱** as a resultative complement indicates the result of eating to fullness: 我**吃饱**了 (I'm full), **吃不饱** (can't get full / not enough to eat). Compare other eating result complements: **吃完** (finished eating), **吃光** (ate it all up), **吃撑** (ate until uncomfortably stuffed). These result complements are central to Chinese verb grammar.
**怀揣着** (huái chuāi zhe) = carrying in one's heart/bosom, harboring: **怀揣** (huái chuāi, to carry close to one's chest, to harbor in the heart) + **着** (zhe, ongoing state marker). **怀揣**着梦想 (carrying a dream in one's heart), **怀揣**着希望 (harboring hope). **着** (zhe) marks an ongoing accompanying state or manner: **带**着笑容 (with a smile), **怀揣**着热情 (with passion in one's heart). This elevated expression adds poetic warmth to the closing image.
